summary: A meditation-centric school of Mahayana Buddhism, known as Chan in China (its point of origin), Seon in Korea, and Thien in Vietnam, and most closely associated in quizbowl with Japan. Traditionally traced to the semi-legendary Indian monk Bodhidharma, who brought the teaching to China and is said to have meditated facing a wall for years, cutting off his own eyelids to stay awake. Its hallmark practices are zazen (seated meditation) and contemplation of koans — paradoxical statements like "Mu" or "the sound of one hand clapping" — compiled in collections such as The Gateless Gate and The Blue Cliff Record, aimed at sudden enlightenment (satori/kensho). Its two dominant Japanese branches are Rinzai (koan-focused, revived by Hakuin Ekaku) and Soto (founded by Dogen, emphasizing "just sitting" shikantaza), alongside the smaller Obaku school. It was carried to the West by figures like D.T. Suzuki, engaged in interfaith dialogue by the Trappist monk Thomas Merton, and given a socially engaged form by Thich Nhat Hanh.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: Standard closing/identifying clues for Zen as a whole, plus its aesthetic legacy, its early history in China, and its 20th-century transmission to the West.

clues.clue: Meditation-centric branch/sect of Mahayana Buddhism founded by (or traced to) Bodhidharma; commonly practiced in Japan (cognate traditions are called Chan in China, Seon in Korea, Thien in Vietnam)
examples: name this Mahayana sect founded by Bodhidharma which is commonly practiced through meditation and is popular in Japan.
For 10 points, name this form of Mahayana Buddhism practiced mostly in Japan.

clues.clue: Goal is satori or kensho, a sudden insight into one's own Buddha-nature
examples: In this tradition, one gains insight into one's own Buddha-nature, or kensho, during a sudden enlightenment called satori.
Achieving insight, or kensho, is the main goal of this religion's Rinzai sect.

clues.clue: Its name derives from the Sanskrit dhyana ("meditation"), transmitted through the Chinese term Chan
examples: The Sanskrit word "Dhyana" is the origin for the name of this religion.

clues.clue: Its early history split into Northern and Southern schools, disagreeing over "gradual" versus "sudden" teachings
examples: This tradition was divided into northern and southern schools that differed in emphasizing "gradual teachings" or "sudden teachings."

clues.clue: Its main surviving Japanese branches/schools are Soto, Rinzai, and Obaku
examples: Name this Buddhist sect that is divided into the Soto, Rinzai, and Obaku schools of thought and whose practitioners are mainly found in Japan.
Its Soto, Rinzai, and Obaku schools study paradoxical koan.

clues.clue: According to legend, the tradition began when the Buddha silently held up a flower before his disciples (the "Flower Sermon"); only Mahakasyapa understood and smiled, receiving a wordless transmission
examples: The Flower Sermon is often cited as an origin story for this school of Buddhism, which was brought to China by Bodhidharma.

clues.clue: Its ink-wash painters, including Shubun and Sesshu Toyo (whose 50-foot four-seasons painting pioneered "broken" and "splashed" styles), introduced the genre to Japan, alongside portraiture called sensho and rock-garden landscaping
examples: An artist from this religion completed a 50-foot painting of the four seasons and developed "broken" and "splashed" styles.

Those Ox-Herding Pictures were created by artists of this religion, who also invented a genre of portraits called sensho and a sculptural form in which rakes are used to create patterns in "rock gardens."

clues.clue: Inspired modern Western artists: John Cage's ten circular ox-taming drawings and Brice Marden's "Zen Studies (Early State)" series; Nam June Paik's blank-screen video work "Zen for Film" (Paik was drawn to the religion by Cage, who was in turn inspired by D.T. Suzuki's lectures)
examples: In one of his forays into visual arts, John Cage imitated artists of this religion by making a series of ten circular drawings depicting the catching and taming of a bull.

A blank image comprises a Nam June Paik work titled for this religion "for Film." Paik was led to this religion by John Cage, whose piece 4'33" was inspired by D. T. Suzuki's lectures on this religion's meditation practice.

clues.clue: 20th-century Kyoto School philosopher Keiji Nishitani (author of Religion and Nothingness) synthesized existentialism with this tradition and its concept of mu ("nothingness")
examples: Keiji Nishitani's thought synthesized existentialism with this branch of Mahayana Buddhism, which focuses on meditation and contemplation of paradoxical koans.
That author, Keiji Nishitani, wrote about the "standpoint" of this school of Buddhism, whose philosophers have often discussed mu, or nothingness.

clues.clue: Miyamoto Musashi's The Book of Five Rings (specifically its "Book of Void") draws on the tradition's philosophy, later analyzed in Eugen Herrigel's Zen and the Art of Archery
examples: Musashi's Book of Void draws on the philosophy of this Mahayana tradition analyzed in "the Art of Archery" by Eugen Herrigel.

clues.clue: Popularized in the West by Philip Kapleau (founded a study center in Rochester, NY; wrote The Three Pillars of Zen) and by Alan Watts, who wrote about its "way" and "spirit"
examples: Philip Kapleau founded a center for the study of this tradition in Rochester, New York and wrote a book about its "three pillars."
Books about the "way" and the "spirit" of this tradition were written by Alan Watts.

clues.clue: San Francisco Zen Center teacher Richard Baker resigned after an affair was revealed, one of several Western Zen sex scandals in the 1980s
examples: A practitioner of this religion named Richard Baker resigned after it was revealed that he was having an affair with the wife of one of his followers, in one of a rash of sex scandals this religion faced in the 1980s.

clues.clue: Popularized in Germany by the psychotherapist Karlfried Graf Durckheim, who developed "initiation therapy"
examples: This religious tradition was popularized in Germany by a Nazi psychotherapist who developed "initiation therapy," Karlfried Graf Durckheim.

clues.clue: Gestalt therapy (developed by Fritz and Laura Perls, taught at the Esalen Institute) blended Western therapeutic ideas with the tradition's introspective meditative practices
examples: Gestalt practice combined western therapeutic ideas with the introspective meditative practices of this eastern religious movement that was popularized in the U.S. by the unrelated writers D. T. Suzuki and Shunryu Suzuki.

clues.clue: Jon Kabat-Zinn adapted the teachings of Thich Nhat Hanh when developing Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction (MBSR)
examples: Kabat-Zinn adapted the teachings of Thich Nhat Hanh in developing the MBSR course. Which religion did these teachings stem from?

clues.clue: Beat Generation writers engaged with the tradition: Jack Kerouac's novel The Dharma Bums follows characters seeking enlightenment (its Japhy Ryder is based on the poet Gary Snyder), and Allen Ginsberg was among the converts
examples: For 10 points, many Beat writers converted to what religion, which inspires the protagonists of Jack Kerouac's The Dharma Bums to try to reach enlightenment?
In a novel written by a follower of this religion, the character of Japhy Ryder is based on Gary Snyder, a poet who followed this religion.

works.name: Bodhidharma
works.description: The semi-legendary Indian (or Central Asian) monk revered as the tradition's founder and first patriarch, remembered as a "blue-eyed barbarian" who carried the teaching from India to China. Central to his legend is a multi-year vigil of wall-facing meditation at the Shaolin Temple, during which he cut off his own eyelids to keep from falling asleep (in some retellings they took root as the first tea plants); he is also credited, apocryphally, with teaching the Shaolin monks the physical forms that became the basis of kung fu. His successor Huike proved his devotion — in one telling by cutting off his own arm, in another simply by bowing and standing in silence — earning Bodhidharma's praise "you have attained my marrow" and the transmission of leadership. He is also traditionally credited as author of the Treatise on the Two Entrances and Four Practices, appears as the subject of several koans in The Gateless Gate, and, according to legend, was buried with a single shoe that followers later found empty in his grave.

clues.clue: "Blue-eyed barbarian" who cut off his eyelids to avoid falling asleep during years of meditation facing a wall at the Shaolin Temple (accounts vary between seven and nine years); in some versions the discarded eyelids grew into the first tea plants
examples: The first tea plants grew after the founder of this school fell asleep while (*) staring at a wall for seven years and cut off his eyelids.
This man is considered the founder of Zen Buddhism. He traveled to China from India and ripped out his eyelids after meditating at Shaolin Temple for nine years.

clues.clue: His successor Huike proved his devotion — either by cutting off his own arm and presenting it, or by silently bowing and sitting down when asked to demonstrate his understanding — after which Bodhidharma declared "you have attained my marrow"
examples: The founder of this tradition said "you have attained my marrow" to his successor, who earlier cut off his arm to show devotion.
This man steadfastly refused to allow Huike to become his apprentice until Huike cut off his own arm and gave it to this man to prove his devotion.

clues.clue: Traditionally credited as author of the Treatise on the Two Entrances and Four Practices
examples: A teacher in this religion is held to be the author of The Treatise on the Two Entrances and Four Practices.
Name this sect founded by the author of the Treatise on the Two Entrances and Four Practices.

clues.clue: Apocryphally taught the monks of the Shaolin Temple 18 forms/kung fu
examples: That "blue-eyed barbarian" who founded this school taught 18 forms to the monks of the Shaolin temple.
This man legendarily taught kung fu to the monks of Shaolin Monastery.

clues.clue: Is a subject of several entries in the koan collection The Gateless Gate
examples: This tradition was founded by Bodhidharma, who is the subject of several entries in the Gateless Gate, a collection of koans.

clues.clue: After his death, followers reportedly found only a single shoe left behind in his grave
examples: A legendary founder of this religious tradition allegedly cut off his eyelids to prevent himself from falling asleep while he stared at a wall for nine years, and after his death left a single shoe for followers to discover in his grave.

clues.clue: When he visited Emperor Wu of China and gave an enigmatic answer, an official claimed the bodhisattva Guanyin was communicating through him
examples: When Bodhidharma visited the Emperor Wu, one of his officials stated that this bodhisattva was communicating through him. This figure has a lot of arms to remove obstacles.

works.name: Huineng
works.description: The tradition's Sixth Patriarch, celebrated for winning a legendary poetry contest against the senior monk Shenxiu: where Shenxiu compared the mind to a mirror-stand needing constant polishing, Huineng countered that since the mind is fundamentally empty, "if a mirror has no stand, where is the dust?" He is said to have achieved sudden enlightenment upon hearing the Diamond Sutra recited, and is traditionally credited as the source of the Platform Sutra, which relates the transmission of the dharma from his own teacher Hongren down to his disciples.

clues.clue: Won a legendary poetry contest against Shenxiu by rejecting his rival's comparison of the mind to a mirror on a stand needing polishing, arguing there is no stand for dust to gather on
examples: In a narrative text from this religion, a spiritual leader is dissatisfied by an entry in a poetry contest that compares the mind to a bright mirror's stand.
In a legendary verse contest, Huineng rejected Shenxiu's analogy that the mind was a mirror stand and the body was this object.

clues.clue: Traditionally considered the Sixth Patriarch, achieving sudden enlightenment after hearing the Diamond Sutra
examples: Name this monk, traditionally considered to be the author of an early "Platform" Sutra. This "Sixth Patriarch" of a certain tradition was said to have achieved "sudden enlightenment" after reading the Diamond Sutra.
One of this religion's scriptures is named after the Platform of its Sixth Patriarch, Huineng.

clues.clue: Credited as the source of the Platform Sutra, which relates the dharma's transmission from his teacher Hongren down to his own disciples
examples: Huineng was the sixth patriarch of this Chinese Buddhist tradition, whose first patriarch was Bodhidharma.
Huineng was the sixth patriarch of this sect of Buddhism, whose members seek enlightenment through personal insight, aided by meditation and contemplation of koans.

works.name: The Platform Sutra
works.description: The tradition's central scripture, unusual among Buddhist sutras for its autobiographical framing: it is presented as the teachings of Huineng, the Sixth Patriarch, and relates the story of the dharma's transmission from his teacher Hongren to himself and onward to his own disciples, incorporating the legendary mirror-versus-mind poetry contest against Shenxiu.

clues.clue: Unusual among sutras for its (apocryphal) autobiographical elements; relates the dharma's transmission from Hongren to Huineng, and from Huineng to his own disciples
examples: Name this sutra that unusually contains autobiographical elements, albeit apocryphal ones, relating the story of the transmission of the dharma from Hongren to Huineng and from Huineng to his disciples.

clues.clue: Named for the "Platform" associated with the Sixth Patriarch Huineng
examples: One of this religion's scriptures is named after the Platform of its Sixth Patriarch, Huineng.

works.name: Koans
works.description: Paradoxical statements, riddles, or dialogues used, especially in the Rinzai school, to short-circuit ordinary logical thought and provoke sudden insight. Famous examples include the monk Zhaozhou's one-word answer "Mu" when asked whether a dog has Buddha-nature, Hakuin Ekaku's koan asking what sound is made by one hand clapping, and the challenge to show one's "original face" from before one's parents were born. Practitioners of the huatou method (introduced by Dahui Zonggao) focus on a single word from a koan, while students also memorize fixed "capping phrases" as philosophical answers. Major koans are compiled in collections including The Gateless Gate and The Blue Cliff Record.

clues.clue: When a monk asked Zhaozhou whether a dog has Buddha-nature, he replied simply "Mu"
examples: A saying from this religious tradition describes the reply "mu" to a question about the nature of a dog.
In a famous koan, the teacher Zhaozhou is asked if a dog has this trait to which he replies, "Mu."

clues.clue: Hakuin Ekaku's koan asks what sound is made by one hand clapping
examples: Students of this religion memorize "capping phrases" as fixed answers to questions used for philosophical understanding in this religion, such as what the sound of one hand clapping is.
The Zen Buddhist thinker Hakuin Ekaku (hah-koo-een ay-kah-koo) formulated a koan that asks what sound this body part makes.

clues.clue: A koan from The Gateless Gate asks the student to show his "original face" — the one he had before his parents were born
examples: Another Zen koan, which originated in the Gateless Gate, asks the listener to show the Zen master his "original" body part of this type, the one that the listener possessed "before your parents were born."

clues.clue: The saying "If you meet the Buddha, kill him" is among the koan parables taught in the tradition (attributed to Linji)
examples: The saying "If you meet the Buddha, kill him" is one of the koan parables taught in what tradition popular in Japan that emphasizes meditation?

clues.clue: The huatou method — focusing on a single word or phrase from a koan — was introduced by the monk Dahui Zonggao; Wumen Huikai's famous commentaries on koans are compiled in The Gateless Gate and The Blue Cliff Record
examples: The practice of focusing on specific words in these texts called huatou was introduced by Dahui Zonggao.
Wumen Huikai made famous commentaries on these texts, which are compiled in (*) The Gateless Gate and The Blue Cliff Record.

works.name: The Gateless Gate
works.description: A koan collection compiled with commentary by the 13th-century monk Wumen Huikai, whose title (also translated "Gateless Barrier") refers to a paradoxical gate that practitioners must pass through, without a gate to pass through, to reach enlightenment. It contains Bodhidharma as the subject of several entries and is the origin of the "original face" koan.

clues.clue: Compiled with commentary by Wumen Huikai; its paradoxical title refers to a gate practitioners must pass through to reach enlightenment, though it has no actual gate
examples: The Chan Buddhist Linji Xiyuan described reality using three "mysterious" examples of these things. Many koans are collected in a book titled for a paradoxically named example of these objects that people must pass to reach enlightenment.
Wumen Huikai made famous commentaries on these texts, which are compiled in (*) The Gateless Gate and The Blue Cliff Record.

clues.clue: Bodhidharma is the subject of several of its entries; it is the source of the "original face" koan
examples: This tradition was founded by Bodhidharma, who is the subject of several entries in the Gateless Gate, a collection of koans.
Another Zen koan, which originated in the Gateless Gate, asks the listener to show the Zen master his "original" body part of this type.

works.name: The Blue Cliff Record
works.description: A major koan anthology compiling paradoxical statements from the Song dynasty, including entries titled "the Highest Meaning of the Holy Truths" and "Hsueh Fang's Grain of Rice." It is frequently mentioned alongside The Gateless Gate as one of the tradition's two foundational koan collections.

clues.clue: Compiles paradoxical koans of the Song Dynasty, including "the Highest Meaning of the Holy Truths" and "Hsueh Fang's Grain of Rice"
examples: Like the Gateless Gate, this book compiles many koans of the Song Dynasty. Classics like "the Highest Meaning of the Holy Truths" and "Hsueh Fang's Grain of Rice" are included in this book.

clues.clue: Named alongside The Gateless Gate as a collection of the tradition's paradoxical koans
examples: This religious tradition produced the Blue Cliff Record and The Gateless Gate.
Several statements important to this religion are collected in the Blue Cliff Record, which contains paradoxical statements known as koans.

works.name: The Ox-Herding Pictures (Ten Bulls)
works.description: A set of ten pedagogical pictures, traditionally attributed to the 12th-century monk Kakuan, depicting a man tracking, catching, taming, and riding an ox as an allegory for the stages of enlightenment; in the sequence the ox and then the herder himself eventually disappear (the eighth picture is famously blank, symbolizing transcendence). The genre inspired modern homages, including a set of ten circular drawings by John Cage imitating the ox-taming imagery.

clues.clue: A set of ten pictures created by Kakuan depicting a man tracking, chasing, and riding an ox that eventually disappears; the eighth picture is blank, symbolizing transcendence
examples: Name this type of animal, which are depicted in that set of 10 pictures created by Kakuan. That set of pictures is a prominent pedagogical tool for reaching enlightenment in Chan Buddhism.
One text central to this faith follows a man variously tracking, chasing, and riding the central animal, which then disappears.

clues.clue: John Cage imitated the imagery in a series of ten circular drawings depicting the catching and taming of a bull
examples: In one of his forays into visual arts, John Cage imitated artists of this religion by making a series of ten circular drawings depicting the catching and taming of a bull.

works.name: Rinzai School
works.description: One of the tradition's two dominant Japanese branches (descended from the Chinese Linji school, whose patriarch Linji Yixuan is known in Japan as Rinzai), historically favored among the samurai class. It emphasizes achieving kensho through sustained contemplation of koans, and was revived in the 18th century by Hakuin Ekaku, who held that serving others was Zen's central concern and who is credited with the "sound of one hand clapping" koan.

clues.clue: Revived by Hakuin Ekaku, who believed serving others was Zen's central concern; particularly practiced among the samurai; derived from the Chinese Linji school
examples: This sect of Zen Buddhism, particularly practiced among samurai, was revived by Hakuin Ekaku ("HA-koo-een AY-ka-koo"), who believed that serving others was the central concern of Zen. It is derived from the Chinese Linji school.

clues.clue: Its patriarch Linji (Chinese: Linji Yixuan) explained the concepts of "mystery in essence," "mystery in words," and "mystery in mystery" as the Three Gates, and is credited with the saying "if you meet the Buddha, kill him"
examples: That patriarch within this tradition is named (*) Linji, who is elsewhere known as Rinzai.

clues.clue: Emphasizes kensho (insight into one's Buddha-nature) attained through meditation on koans
examples: Achieving insight, or kensho, is the main goal of this religion's Rinzai sect.
The Rinzai sect of this school emphasizes kensho, a form of self-awareness attained through satori.
Adherents of its Rinzai school meditate on koans to attain enlightenment.

works.name: Dogen and the Soto School
works.description: Dogen Zenji (also called Eihei Dogen or Kigen Dogen), founder of the Soto school — the tradition's largest branch in Japan. He taught that all sentient beings, including plants, innately possess Buddha-nature, and promoted shikantaza ("just sitting"), a form of silent meditation without active contemplation of koans, in his treatise the Bendowa. He compiled some 301 koans in his major work The True Dharma Eye (Shobogenzo).

clues.clue: Founded the Soto school, the largest Zen sect in Japan; wrote The True Dharma Eye and promoted shikantaza, silent "just sitting" meditation without active contemplation of koans
examples: The largest Zen Buddhist sect in Japan, the Soto school, was founded by this author of The True Dharma Eye. He emphasized shikantaza ("shee-kahn-tah-zah"), or silent meditation without contemplation of koans.

This Zen teacher supported the idea that all living things, including plants, have Buddha-Nature. He compiled 301 koans in The True Dharma Eye, promoted shikantaza meditation, and founded the Soto school.

clues.clue: Taught that all living things, including plants, possess Buddha-nature
examples: This Zen teacher supported the idea that all living things, including plants, have Buddha-Nature.

clues.clue: Wrote the Bendowa, promoting the practice of zazen
examples: The founder of a school of this religion wrote the Bendowa in which he promoted the practice of (*) zazen.

works.name: D.T. Suzuki
works.description: Suzuki Daisetsu Teitaro, the Japanese scholar most responsible for popularizing Zen in the West through his English-language writings, including a 1934 Introduction to Zen Buddhism and the essay "Zen and the Samurai" in Zen and Japanese Culture. He compared Zen concepts like satori and sunyata to the thought of the Christian mystic Meister Eckhart, engaged in interfaith dialogue with the Trappist monk Thomas Merton (published as "Wisdom in Emptiness"), and inspired John Cage's engagement with Zen through his lectures on meditation.

clues.clue: The scholar most responsible for popularizing Zen in the West; compared its concepts (satori, sunyata) to the Christian mystic Meister Eckhart
examples: This tradition was largely popularized in the west by D. T. Suzuki, who drew parallels between it and the thought of Meister Eckhart.
Name this writer, who compared the thought of Meister Eckhart to concepts such as satori and sunyata in his introductions to an Asian religious school.

clues.clue: Wrote "Zen and the Samurai" in Zen and Japanese Culture and a 1934 Introduction to Zen Buddhism; distinct from the unrelated musical pedagogue Shinichi Suzuki
examples: A thinker with this surname wrote "Zen and the Samurai" in Zen and Japanese Culture, and penned a 1934 Introduction to Zen Buddhism. Nurtured by Love contains the philosophy of a musical pedagogue of this surname.

clues.clue: Engaged in interfaith dialogue with the Trappist monk Thomas Merton, published as the essay "Wisdom in Emptiness"
examples: This man's dialogue with Catholic writer Thomas Merton was published as the essay "Wisdom in Emptiness."

clues.clue: His lectures on Zen inspired John Cage's engagement with the tradition, which in turn led to Nam June Paik's "Zen for Film"
examples: Paik was led to this religion by John Cage, whose piece 4'33" was inspired by D. T. Suzuki's lectures on this religion's meditation practice.

works.name: Thomas Merton
works.description: The American Trappist monk and author of the autobiography The Seven Storey Mountain, who studied Zen and other Eastern traditions in dialogues with D.T. Suzuki and the Confucian-trained Catholic convert John C.H. Wu, and discussed Christ's kenosis in relation to Zen in his book Zen and the Birds of Appetite.

clues.clue: Wrote the autobiography The Seven Storey Mountain; studied Zen in dialogues with D.T. Suzuki and the Confucian-trained Catholic convert John C.H. Wu
examples: This Trappist monk studied Zen Buddhism and other Eastern traditions in dialogues with D.T. Suzuki and the Confucian-trained Catholic convert John C.H. Wu. This monk's autobiography is titled The Seven-Storey Mountain.

clues.clue: Discussed Christ's kenosis (Philippians 2:7) in relation to Zen in his book Zen and the Birds of Appetite
examples: A Trappist monk discussed Christ's kenosis in Philippians 2:7 in terms of this tradition in the book [this tradition] and the Birds of Appetite.

works.name: Thich Nhat Hanh
works.description: The Vietnamese Thien (Zen) Buddhist monk who, alongside B.R. Ambedkar, helped establish the socially and politically active Engaged Buddhism movement. He founded the Plum Village Monastery in France after being banished from Vietnam, and his teachings were later adapted by Jon Kabat-Zinn in developing Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction (MBSR).

clues.clue: Helped establish Engaged Buddhism alongside B.R. Ambedkar; founded the Plum Village Monastery in France after being banished from his home country
examples: This Thien ("TEE-in") Buddhist monk helped establish Engaged Buddhism along with B. R. Ambedkar. This monk founded the Plum Village Monastery in France after he was banished from his home country.

clues.clue: His teachings were adapted by Jon Kabat-Zinn in developing Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction (MBSR)
examples: Kabat-Zinn adapted the teachings of Thich Nhat Hanh in developing the MBSR course.

comprehensive_summary: Zen Buddhism is a meditation-focused school of Mahayana Buddhism known by the cognate names Chan (in its country of origin, China), Seon (Korea), and Thien (Vietnam), though quizbowl clues overwhelmingly frame it through its Japanese form. Tradition credits its founding to the semi-legendary Indian monk Bodhidharma, remembered as a "blue-eyed barbarian" who meditated facing a wall at the Shaolin Temple for seven to nine years, cutting off his own eyelids to avoid sleep (which in some tellings grew into the first tea plants) and apocryphally teaching the monks there the martial forms behind kung fu. His successor Huike proved his devotion — by cutting off his own arm in one telling, or by silently bowing in another — earning the praise "you have attained my marrow." The lineage's Sixth Patriarch, Huineng, is celebrated for besting the monk Shenxiu in a poetry contest by rejecting his comparison of the mind to a mirror needing polishing, and is traditionally credited as the source of the Platform Sutra, the tradition's central (apocryphally autobiographical) scripture. Core practice centers on zazen, seated meditation, and on koans — paradoxical statements such as Zhaozhou's "Mu," Hakuin Ekaku's "sound of one hand clapping," and the challenge to reveal one's "original face" — compiled in anthologies including The Gateless Gate (compiled by Wumen Huikai) and The Blue Cliff Record, aimed at sudden enlightenment, called satori or kensho. A parallel pedagogical tradition, the Ox-Herding Pictures (Ten Bulls), allegorizes the stages of enlightenment through a herder's pursuit of a vanishing ox. The tradition's two dominant living Japanese branches are Rinzai, descended from the Chinese Linji school and revived by Hakuin Ekaku, historically popular among the samurai and centered on koan contemplation; and Soto, the largest branch, founded by Dogen Zenji, who taught that all beings possess Buddha-nature and promoted shikantaza ("just sitting") in his treatise the Bendowa and his major work The True Dharma Eye. A smaller Obaku school completes the trio. In the 20th century the tradition was carried west chiefly through D.T. Suzuki, who compared its concepts to the Christian mystic Meister Eckhart and engaged in interfaith dialogue with the Trappist monk Thomas Merton (author of Zen and the Birds of Appetite and The Seven Storey Mountain); other Western popularizers include Alan Watts and Philip Kapleau, while the Vietnamese monk Thich Nhat Hanh developed the socially engaged "Engaged Buddhism" movement and influenced Jon Kabat-Zinn's Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction. Zen's aesthetic legacy includes ink-wash painting (Shubun, Sesshu Toyo), rock gardens, and homages by modern Western artists John Cage, Brice Marden, and Nam June Paik, while its philosophy influenced Miyamoto Musashi's The Book of Five Rings and, in the 20th century, the Kyoto School philosopher Keiji Nishitani.
recursive_suggestions: Bodhidharma — dense enough on his own (Shaolin legend, Huike succession, Treatise, Emperor Wu) to merit a standalone Religion page; watch overlap with this page's Figure section
D.T. Suzuki — recurring figure with enough independent material (Eckhart comparisons, Merton dialogue, Cage connection) for his own page
Dogen — could support a standalone page (Shobogenzo/True Dharma Eye, Bendowa, shikantaza) if more clues surface; currently folded in with the Soto school here
Thomas Merton — recommend a Religion or possibly Literature category filter if pursued independently; his Catholic-Zen dialogue is a recurring bonus topic
Miyamoto Musashi / The Book of Five Rings — appeared as its own bonus part answerline; recommend Literature or History category filter
Pure Land Buddhism (Jodo bukkyo) and Shingon Buddhism — appeared as sibling parts in bonuses about "major schools of Japanese Buddhism" but are distinct traditions, not part of Zen; do not conflate
Wabi-sabi / mono no aware — recurring aesthetic concepts described as derived from Zen philosophy; could support a standalone Fine Arts or Philosophy page

Keiji Nishitani / Kyoto School — appeared mainly as a Philosophy-category bonus answerline (Religion and Nothingness, Threefold Topology); recommend Philosophy category filter if pursued independently

Flag ambiguous/general 'Buddhism' answerline: several tossups (Beat Generation, The Buddha in the Attic, Lincoln in the Bardo) accept Zen Buddhism as an alternate but are really about Buddhism broadly
